main_ingredients: whole fish (seabass / siakap or red snapper)

serving.description: 1 serving (approximately 300 g, including fish and sauce)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Ikan Masak Tiga Rasa is approximately 300 g, consisting of a portion of deep-fried whole fish (seabass or red snapper) generously coated with the sweet, sour, and spicy three-flavor sauce, as commonly served at Malaysian and Thai communal meals.

nutrient_source.title: Nutrient Composition of Southeast Asian Sweet and Sour Fish Dishes — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values adapted from HPB Singapore food composition data for sweet-and-sour fish dishes and deep-fried fish with sauce, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entries for seabass (raw) and sweet-sour sauce components. The input record cited HPB Singapore as the primary source; values are retained and validated as plausible for this dish type. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 1.8 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central data for deep-fried seabass with sauce (SR Legacy ID 15076 and sauce component analogy).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for sweet-sour fish dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Ikan Masak Tiga Rasa. GI estimated at 55 based on analogous sweet-and-sour fish dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category (<=55) per the University of Sydney classification. The sauce contains significant simple sugars (chili sauce, tomato sauce, plum sauce, added sugar, pineapple) which elevate GI above plain fish. The high protein and fat content of the fish moderates the overall GI. Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI x carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (55 x 33.6) / 100 = 18.5.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (380 mg/100 g; approximately 1140 mg per 300 g serving) from multiple sauces and deep-frying
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150 g, request low-sodium sauce variants, and avoid adding extra salt or soy sauce, as a standard 300 g serving provides approximately 57% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load (11.2 g/100 g) with high sugar content (8.5 g/100 g) from sweet sauces; GI approximately 55 (low-medium boundary)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a reduced portion (150-200 g), minimize sauce intake to lower the glycaemic load, and pair the dish with non-starchy vegetables and a low-GI carbohydrate staple to blunt postprandial blood glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying increases total fat exposure; high sodium; however, fish provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional servings, prefer steamed or grilled fish preparations where possible, and be aware that the deep-frying process and high-sodium sauce partially offset the cardiovascular benefits of the omega-3-rich fish.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Fish provides anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids; ginger and bromelain (pineapple) may reduce inflammation; however, deep-frying and high sugar content may be pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the omega-3 content of the fish and the anti-inflammatory properties of ginger and pineapple, but should limit portion size and sauce quantity to reduce pro-inflammatory sugar and oxidized fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ikan Masak Tiga Rasa, meaning 'Three-Flavor Cooked Fish' in Malay, is a celebrated Southeast Asian main dish popular in Malaysia and Thailand (where it is known as Pla Sam Rot, ปลาสามรส). The dish features a whole fish — typically seabass (siakap) or red snapper — that is deep-fried until the skin is shatteringly crispy and golden. The fish is then smothered in a vibrant, sticky sauce that harmonizes three distinct flavor profiles: sweetness from sugar, honey, or sweet chili sauce; sourness from tamarind, vinegar, or plum sauce; and heat from fresh or dried chilies. The sauce is built by stir-frying aromatics including shallots, garlic, ginger, and lemongrass before adding chili sauce, tomato sauce, and plum sauce. Chopped pineapple, tomato, cucumber, and onion are often folded in to add texture and freshness. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 185 kcal, 14.2 g protein, 9.5 g fat, and 11.2 g carbohydrate, with 8.5 g sugar and 380 mg sodium. The high sodium content (approximately 1140 mg per standard 300 g serving) is a concern for individuals with hypertension. The GI of approximately 55 (low-medium boundary) and significant sugar content from the sauce make portion control important for people with Type 2 Diabetes. The fish itself contributes beneficial omega-3 fatty acids, selenium (18 mcg/100 g), niacin (3.8 mg/100 g), and vitamin B12 (1.2 mcg/100 g). The dish is a festive and celebratory food, commonly served at family gatherings and communal dinners across Malaysia, Singapore, and Thailand.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced from HPB Singapore food composition data for analogous sweet-and-sour fish dishes and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central for seabass and sauce components. No exact published nutrient analysis for Ikan Masak Tiga Rasa as a composite dish was found in a single approved source; values represent a well-supported adaptation. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation and moisture data for this composite deep-fried fish with sweet-sour-spicy sauce are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific dish preparation as of 2024; these fields are set to -1 to indicate data not available rather than confirmed zero. saturated_fat_g (1.8 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for deep-fried seabass and sweet-sour sauce analogy; not directly measured for this dish. GI value of 55 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database for sweet-and-sour fish dishes; no direct GI measurement for Ikan Masak Tiga Rasa exists in the approved GI literature. GI of 55 is classified as 'low' per the University of Sydney standard threshold of <=55 for the low category. This dish is classified as Malay/Thai Fusion; multilingual names are provided for Malay, Thai, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Tamil, and Hindi in accordance with the Southeast Asian multilingual coverage policy. Halal status is marked true as the dish is traditionally prepared with halal fish and ingredients; consumers should verify sauce ingredients for halal certification. All strings present in aliases_by_language are confirmed to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array.
